messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

The honest answer is that nobody's quite sure — hiccups seem to be a leftover reflex from an earlier stage of evolution, possibly inherited from gill-breathing ancestors where a similar spasm helped push water over gills.

What's clear is that the diaphragm contracts suddenly and the vocal cords snap shut a fraction of a second later — that's the sound. But there's no obvious survival benefit for modern humans, which is why most researchers treat it as evolutionary baggage.

Does the "why do we still have useless things" angle interest you, or was it more the mechanics you were curious about?
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

That's a very relatable rabbit hole — one person's hiccups and suddenly you're reading about evolutionary biology.

How long is "forever" for a seven-year-old? Because there's a point where hiccups that just won't quit actually do warrant a look from a doctor, though most cases resolve on their own.
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The folk cure collection is genuinely fascinating — sugar, holding your breath, being startled, drinking upside down. They're all doing roughly the same thing: trying to interrupt or reset that diaphragm spasm, just through wildly different routes.

The sugar one actually has a small amount of evidence behind it, which probably explains why it stuck around in your grandmother's repertoire.
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

It's a good reminder that "common" and "well understood" aren't the same thing — yawning is in a similar boat, surprisingly.

There's something almost reassuring about it, that the body still has corners science hasn't fully mapped.
